name: Load Balancer
definition: Distributes incoming requests across many identical servers so no single instance is overwhelmed, and routes around dead ones.
cons: Can become a bottleneck/SPOF if not redundant
whenToUse: Any service that runs on more than one machine and needs traffic spread evenly.
whenNotToUse: A single-instance internal tool, or when the real bottleneck is the database, not the app tier.

name: CDN (Content Delivery Network)
definition: A geographically distributed cache of edge servers that serves static/media content from a location near the user.
pros: Absorbs traffic spikes for static assets
cons: Useless for highly dynamic, per-user data
whenToUse: Serving images, video, JS/CSS, or any cacheable blob to a global audience.
whenNotToUse: Personalized, frequently-changing API responses that can't be cached.

name: In-Memory Cache (Redis/Memcached)
definition: A fast key-value store in RAM that holds hot data so you avoid recomputing or re-reading from a slower database.
pros: Takes load off the primary DB
whenToUse: Read-heavy access patterns with a hot subset of data (sessions, counters, feeds, lookups).
whenNotToUse: Write-heavy data with low re-read rate, or when you need durable storage.

name: Read Replica
definition: A copy of the database that asynchronously follows the primary and serves read queries to scale read throughput.
pros: Isolates analytics from the write path
whenToUse: Read:write ratio is high and you can tolerate slightly stale reads.
whenNotToUse: Write-bound systems, or when every read must be perfectly fresh.

name: Sharding / Partitioning
definition: Splitting one logical dataset across many database nodes by a partition key so each node holds only a slice.
pros: Scales writes and storage past one machine
whenToUse: A single DB can no longer hold the data or absorb the write volume.
whenNotToUse: Data fits comfortably on one node — sharding early adds needless complexity.

name: Message Queue
definition: A durable buffer (e.g. SQS, RabbitMQ) that decouples producers from consumers so work is processed asynchronously.
whenToUse: Slow or spiky background work: sending email, encoding video, fan-out, ingestion.
whenNotToUse: Synchronous request/response where the user needs the answer right now.

name: Pub/Sub (Kafka/PubSub)
definition: A publish-subscribe log where one event is broadcast to many independent consumer groups, often replayable.
whenToUse: Event-driven fan-out, activity streams, log/event pipelines, change propagation.
whenNotToUse: Simple point-to-point task hand-off (a plain queue is enough).

name: Rate Limiter
definition: Caps how many requests a client can make in a window (token bucket, sliding window) to protect the system.
whenToUse: Public APIs, login endpoints, or any shared resource that needs protection from spikes/abuse.
whenNotToUse: Purely internal, trusted, low-volume calls where throttling adds no value.

name: API Gateway
definition: A single entry point that handles auth, routing, rate limiting, and request shaping in front of many microservices.
whenToUse: Microservice architectures needing shared auth, routing, throttling at the edge.
whenNotToUse: A small monolith with one public endpoint.

name: Reverse Proxy
definition: A server (e.g. Nginx) that sits in front of app servers to terminate TLS, cache, compress, and forward requests.
whenToUse: Terminating TLS, serving static files, and basic routing in front of app servers.
whenNotToUse: When you already have a full API gateway covering these concerns.

name: NoSQL Key-Value / Wide-Column Store
definition: A schemaless store (DynamoDB, Cassandra) optimized for fast lookups by key and massive horizontal scale.
cons: You design around access patterns up front
whenToUse: Huge scale, simple access-by-key patterns: user profiles, sessions, mappings, feeds.
whenNotToUse: Complex relational queries, transactions across entities, strong ACID needs.

name: Relational Database (SQL)
definition: A schema-based store with ACID transactions and joins (Postgres, MySQL) — strong consistency by default.
whenToUse: Money, orders, relationships, anything needing transactions and correctness.
whenNotToUse: Internet-scale, simple key lookups where a NoSQL store scales more cheaply.

name: Consistent Hashing
definition: A hashing scheme that maps keys to nodes on a ring so adding/removing a node moves only a small fraction of keys.
whenToUse: Distributing keys across a cache/DB cluster that grows and shrinks.
whenNotToUse: A fixed, small node count where simple hashing is fine.

name: Bloom Filter
definition: A compact probabilistic set that answers 'definitely not present' or 'maybe present' to skip expensive lookups.
whenToUse: Cheaply checking 'have I seen this URL/key?' before a costly read (crawlers, caches, dedupe).
whenNotToUse: When you need exact membership or to retrieve the value itself.

name: Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
definition: Append-only log of changes written before applying them, enabling crash recovery and replication.
whenToUse: Building durable storage, replication, or event sourcing on top of an ordered log.
whenNotToUse: Ephemeral data where durability doesn't matter.

name: Object / Blob Storage (S3)
definition: Durable, cheap storage for large immutable blobs (images, video, backups) addressed by key over HTTP.
whenToUse: Storing user uploads, media, logs, backups — anything large and file-like.
whenNotToUse: Small structured records you need to query or update transactionally.

name: Search Index (Inverted Index / Elasticsearch)
definition: A term→documents index that powers fast full-text search, filtering, and ranking.
cons: Eventually consistent with source of truth
whenToUse: Full-text search, autocomplete corpora, log search, complex filtering.
whenNotToUse: Exact key lookups or as a system of record.

name: WebSocket / Long-Lived Connection
definition: A persistent bidirectional connection so the server can push to the client instantly without polling.
whenToUse: Chat, live location, presence, notifications, collaborative editing.
whenNotToUse: Infrequent updates where simple polling or SSE is cheaper to operate.

name: Geospatial Index (Quadtree / Geohash)
definition: A spatial data structure that answers 'what's near this lat/long?' efficiently.
whenToUse: Find nearby drivers/restaurants/users, maps, location-based matching.
whenNotToUse: Non-spatial data — it adds no value.

name: Consensus / Leader Election (Raft, Paxos)
definition: A protocol that lets a cluster agree on one value/leader despite failures, underpinning strong consistency.
pros: Single source of truth under failure
whenToUse: Coordinating metadata, leader election, config, distributed locks (ZooKeeper/etcd).
whenNotToUse: High-throughput data-plane writes that don't need global agreement.

name: Replication (Leader–Follower)
definition: Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes for durability, read scaling, and failover.
whenToUse: Almost any stateful system that must survive a machine dying.
whenNotToUse: Truly disposable, easily-recomputed data.

name: Eventual Consistency
definition: A model where replicas converge over time; reads may briefly see stale data in exchange for availability and scale.
whenToUse: Likes, view counts, feeds, presence — where being slightly stale is fine.
whenNotToUse: Balances, inventory, bookings — where stale data causes real errors.

name: Strong Consistency
definition: Every read reflects the latest committed write (linearizability), usually via quorums or a single leader.
whenToUse: Payments, seat/inventory reservation, account balances, anything that must not double-spend.
whenNotToUse: Social/feed data where availability and speed matter more than freshness.

name: Change Data Capture (CDC)
definition: Streams row-level changes out of a database (via its log) to keep caches, search indexes, and warehouses in sync.
whenToUse: Keeping a search index, cache, or analytics store in sync with the primary DB.
whenNotToUse: When a simple synchronous write or cache invalidation suffices.

name: Fan-out (on write vs on read)
definition: A feed-delivery strategy: push a new post into each follower's feed (write) or assemble feeds at read time (read).
cons: Read fan-out is slow for big follow lists
whenToUse: Designing news feeds, timelines, notifications with follower graphs.
whenNotToUse: Systems without a one-to-many distribution pattern.

name: Distributed ID Generator (Snowflake)
definition: Generates unique, roughly time-ordered IDs across many machines without a central bottleneck.
whenToUse: Short URLs, tweet/message IDs, any sharded system needing unique sortable keys.
whenNotToUse: Single-node apps where an auto-increment column is enough.

name: Circuit Breaker
definition: Stops calling a failing dependency after an error threshold, failing fast and giving it time to recover.
whenToUse: Calls to flaky external services or downstream dependencies.
whenNotToUse: Local, in-process calls that can't fail in the same way.

name: Idempotency Key
definition: A client-supplied unique token so retried requests are applied at most once (no duplicate charges/records).
whenToUse: Payments, order creation, webhook handlers, any non-idempotent write that may be retried.
whenNotToUse: Naturally idempotent operations (pure PUT/GET).

name: Data Warehouse / OLAP
definition: A columnar analytics store (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) for large aggregate queries, separate from the transactional DB.
whenToUse: Reporting, dashboards, analytics on event/transaction history.
whenNotToUse: Serving individual user requests in real time.

name: Time-Series Database
definition: A store optimized for timestamped metrics with downsampling and retention (Prometheus, InfluxDB).
whenToUse: Monitoring, metrics, surge/demand signals, rate measurements over time.
whenNotToUse: Relational/transactional data.
